questionText: A cyclist rides along a track whose position in metres after $t$ seconds is modelled by $x = 3t$ and $y = t^{2}$. Find the equation of the normal to the track at the instant $t = 2$, giving your answer in the form $ax + by + c = 0$.

steps.explanation: The normal is perpendicular to the tangent, so first we find the tangent gradient, then flip and negate it, and use the point where $t = 2$.

steps.description: Differentiate x with respect to t
steps.explanation: The horizontal velocity is constant here, which will be the denominator of the gradient.

steps.description: Differentiate y with respect to t
steps.explanation: The vertical velocity grows with time; it is the numerator of the gradient.

steps.description: Form the gradient of the path
steps.explanation: Dividing the vertical rate by the horizontal rate gives the slope of the track as a function of $t$.
The normal passes through the cyclist's actual position, so substitute $t = 2$ to get the point $(6,\,4)$.
This is the slope of the track at that instant; the normal must be perpendicular to it.
Perpendicular gradients multiply to $-1$, so we take the negative reciprocal of $\dfrac{4}{3}$.

steps.description: Write the normal in point-slope form
steps.explanation: A straight line through $(6,\,4)$ with gradient $-\dfrac{3}{4}$ is written in point-slope form ready to tidy.
Multiplying through by $4$ removes the fraction so we can collect terms neatly.
Bringing every term to one side gives the requested $ax + by + c = 0$ form.
This line has gradient $-\dfrac{3}{4}$ and passes through $(6,\,4)$, so it is the normal to the track at $t = 2$.

workedSolution.finalAnswer: The normal to the track at $t = 2$ is $3x + 4y - 34 = 0$ (equivalently $y = -\dfrac{3}{4}x + \dfrac{17}{2}$).
